nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers and their tributaries; sandy and muddy substrates with submerged wood and leaf litter; main Amazon channel, Orinoco basin, and Río Madeira drainage

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and robust rockwork
heavy driftwood and submerged logs for shelter and grazing
fine sand or smooth-gravel substrate (protects ventral scutes)
robust or artificial plants only — powerful body will uproot delicate plants
open swimming corridors along tank length
overhangs and caves for daytime retreats

diet.notes: Opportunistic bottom-feeder with a strong preference for molluscs — crushes snail shells with powerful pharyngeal teeth (hence ‘Mother of Snails’). Accepts sinking carnivore pellets, large wafers, frozen mussel, prawn, earthworms, and whole snails. Also takes sinking cichlid sticks and catfish tablets. Feed at night or at lights-out; confirm food reaches the bottom past faster mid-water tankmates. Juveniles accept smaller pellets and chopped invertebrates. Avoid excessive protein-only diets; supplement with plant-based wafers for gut health.

goodWith: large characins (Silver Dollars, Pacu — in proportionally large systems)
other large Doradidae of similar size
avoidWith: any snails or small shrimp (will be consumed)
small fish that fit in its mouth
aggressive large cichlids that may harass a slow-moving catfish
delicate or slow fish unable to avoid its bulk
fish requiring pristine, heavily planted, low-flow setups

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Megalodoras uranoscopus. Shows adult lateral view with characteristic bony lateral scutes and robust body profile.

summary: One of the largest armored catfishes in the hobby, reaching 60–100 cm over a 20+ year lifespan. Its crushing pharyngeal teeth dispatch snails effortlessly. Pectoral spines lock erect and can pierce nets and hands. Needs a 1500 L+ specialist setup.
careNotes:
The Giant Raphael Catfish is among the most massive members of the family Doradidae and one of the largest catfishes encountered in the aquarium trade. Adults routinely exceed 60 cm and specimens approaching 100 cm have been documented in the wild; even in aquaria, 70–80 cm adults are not uncommon after a decade or more of slow growth. A minimum footprint of 300 × 100 cm (approximately 1500 L) is the practical floor; larger is strongly preferred. Filtration must be oversized — a single adult produces substantial waste, and turnover of 8–10× tank volume per hour is recommended.

The species is covered in lateral bony ‘scutes’ (rows of bony plates along the flanks), a characteristic of all Doradidae, and sports sharp, serrated pectoral-fin spines that can lock erect. These spines are capable of piercing fish-nets, puncturing hands, and becoming entangled in mesh; always use a solid container (bucket) rather than a net when moving specimens. Handle with thick gloves and support the body mass.

Megalodoras uranoscopus is primarily nocturnal, spending daylight hours sheltering under logs or large rocks. It accepts food after lights-out; use sinking pellets or whole invertebrates placed near its shelter. The common name ‘Mother of Snails’ reflects its extraordinary capacity to consume snails — the pharyngeal teeth crush shells effortlessly, making it an effective (if overkill) snail-control agent. It will also consume small crustaceans and any fish small enough to engulf.

Water chemistry is flexible: the Amazon basin presents soft, acidic blackwater in some regions and whitewater (moderate hardness) in others; pH 6.0–7.8 and gH 2–20 are all tolerated. Temperature 22–28 °C. Excellent water quality is more important than specific chemistry; large water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential given the fish’s size and waste load.

Juveniles (10–15 cm) are frequently sold in the trade and appear manageable, but buyers must plan for an adult exceeding a metre. Growth is slow but relentless over a 20–30 year lifespan — this is a multi-decade commitment. Rehoming adult specimens is extremely difficult. Do not acquire without a confirmed long-term plan for an appropriately sized system.

Distinguish from the Striped Raphael Catfish (Platydoras armatulus, max ~24 cm) by the absence of bold pale lateral stripes and by the dramatically larger adult size. Juvenile Giant Raphaels may show faint mottling that disappears with age, and body shape alone at small sizes can cause confusion.
breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding reports exist for Megalodoras uranoscopus in home aquaria. In the wild the species is a seasonal breeder tied to Amazon flood cycles, with spawning believed to occur in flooded forest (igapó) margins during the high-water season. Large size, the requirement for flood-cycle triggers (temperature drop, barometric change, significant water-volume increase), and the sheer logistics of housing multiple adult specimens make captive reproduction effectively impossible outside of large public-aquarium facilities with dedicated breeding programmes. Captive breeding difficulty is classified as not in captivity for practical hobby purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Megalodoras uranoscopus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to ~100 cm), temperature range (22–28 °C), pH (6.0–7.8), hardness tolerance, native range (Amazon basin, Orinoco, Río Madeira), habitat notes (large rivers, sandy/muddy substrates), diet (molluscs, invertebrates, plant matter), pectoral-spine locking behaviour, nocturnal habits, and minimum tank guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Megalodoras uranoscopus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Doradidae, Siluriformes), maximum size (100 cm TL), native range (Amazon and Orinoco drainages), temperature (22–28 °C), and diet classification. Confirmed longevity data and mollusc-specialist diet description.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Catfish & Bottom Dwellers care guides
additional.notes: Consulted for general large-catfish husbandry framing (filtration turnover, sinking-food strategy, substrate preferences). M. uranoscopus not covered individually; applied category-level guidance for large Doradidae. Tank-size and water-change recommendations aligned with SeriouslyFish primary data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Doradidae (thorny catfishes) guide
additional.notes: Supplementary source for Doradidae family overview: lateral scute description, pectoral-spine locking mechanism and handler injury risk, nocturnal feeding behaviour, snail-crushing pharyngeal teeth, and notes on distinguishing Megalodoras from Platydoras by size and lack of lateral stripe.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Giant South American Catfishes
additional.notes: Supplementary reference for adult-size expectations, slow growth trajectory, lifespan (20+ years), rehoming difficulties, and the recommendation to plan for 1500 L+ long-term. Also noted the ‘Mother of Snails’ common name origin and the practical near-impossibility of captive breeding in home settings.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in home aquaria. Seasonal Amazon flood-cycle triggers required; sheer size and logistics preclude home breeding. Public-aquarium-scale facilities only.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60–100 cm; massive armored Doradidae with pectoral spines that lock erect and can pierce nets/handler. Needs 1500 L+ tanks. Specialist long-term commitment.
